result:
I’ve been thinking about the way plastic bags sound when they’re caught in the wind. Not the flapping—everyone notices that—but the high, papery whisper that happens just before the handles tangle around a branch and the whole thing goes still. It’s the voice of something that will outlive every witness to its flight. A ghost that hasn’t died yet.

Last week I found a single Lego brick in the garden, half-buried under last year’s leaves. It was the exact cyan of a 1994 Caribbean sky, the one I saw from the balcony of a hotel room when I was eight and believed the ocean had been dyed for tourists. I rinsed the brick in the kitchen sink, watched soil spiral down the drain like weak coffee, and felt time collapse into something the size of a 2×4 stud. I don’t even remember owning that color. Maybe the house itself is quietly growing toys the way trees grow fungus.

Sometimes I think the internet is a compost heap: every thought we toss out there stays warm, stays wet, keeps breaking down until it’s unrecognizable, but never quite gone. A post you regretted at nineteen is still warming some corner of a server farm, exhaling methane in the form of cached data. We bury our digital garbage and pretend it turns into soil, but it’s more like vitrified waste glass—sharp, eternal, and useless to anything alive.

I keep a notebook of obsolete sounds. The clack of a rotary dial returning to zero. The gasp of a VHS tape being ejected. The Nokia mosquito-ringtone that once drifted from every back pocket in a high-school hallway. None of these sounds is more than twenty years gone, yet hearing them now feels like pressing sore gums against the hole where a tooth used to be—an absence with a shape. I wonder what the absence will be when today’s infants are middle-aged: the hush of an internal-combustion engine at a red light? The soft thud of a paperback’s pages closing?

Yesterday the streetlights flickered for a moment, and every screen in the living room blinked in sympathy—laptop, phone, television, smart bulb—all of them gasping like fish in the same shallow puddle of electricity. In that half-second I felt how tethered we are to the same unlit river. When the power finally goes, we’ll discover which memories were only ever stored in light.

I keep meaning to plant something that takes fifty years to bloom. A copper beech, maybe, or one of those Chinese chestnuts that need to feel a few hard frosts before they trust the ground. I’d like to send a message forward that isn’t made of code or plastic or pixels—something that will still be unfolding when every password I’ve ever invented is compost and every cloud has evaporated into someone else’s sky.

Tonight the moon looks like a fingernail clipping, and I’m out on the porch listening for Lego-colored oceans, for plastic bags that still haven’t landed, for the small click a future summer will make when it finally finds anchor in the soil I’m only borrowing.
